coredata.dc:title: Next-generation 3D tumor modeling: A microfluidic platform with biocompatible red carbon dots for live cell imaging in co-cultured elongated spheroid tumor model
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Pournemat, Parisa
Bagheri, Zeinab
Behroodi, Ebrahim
Soleimani, Marziye
Latifi, Hamid
Mayadani, Shadi
Yaghoubi-Avini, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: Co-culture spheroids mimic tumor architecture more accurately than traditional 2D cell cultures, but non-invasive, long-term tracking of live cells within these 3D models remains a challenge. This study addresses this critical need by developing a novel approach for live cell imaging in U-87/HUF co-culture spheroids. We introduce water-soluble, biocompatible red carbon dots (R-CDs) exhibiting exceptional stability and brightness (21% quantum yield) specifically designed for imaging within these 3D models. Furthermore, we designed a microfluidic chip with ellipsoid-shaped microwells to efficiently generate two distinct co-culture spheroid types: direct mixing and core-shell. R-CDs enabled non-invasive tracking of U-87 cancer cell location within these 3D models demonstrating their efficacy for long-term monitoring of live cells in cancer research. This R-CD and microfluidic technology has the potential to accelerate cancer drug discovery by enabling live cell studies in 3D tumor models.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Live cell imaging
Red emission carbon dots
In-vitro imaging
Co-culture ellipsoids
Microfluidic chips

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Live-cell imaging of monolayers using R-CDs
Cell tracking in direct mixing co-culture ellipsoids using R-CDs as the fluorescent prob
Cell tracking in core-shell U-87/HUF ellipsoids using R-CDs as the fluorescent prob

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of the synthesis and purification process for red carbon dots via a solvothermal method and subsequent purification steps, including washing, centrifugation, filtration, and dialysis.
Schematic of co-culture ellipsoid formation process. A) direct mixing and B) core-shell co-culture ellipsoid.

Characterization of R-CDs. (A) fluorescence R-CDs under UV radiation and visible light. (B) Optical spectra of UV–Vis absorption. (C) Emission of R-CDs at different wavelengths. (D) FT-IR spectrum of R-CDs. (E) R-CDs XRD diagram. (F) R-CDs HR-TEM microscopy images with different magnification, respectively left to right crystallization zoon, histogram graph of nanoparticles average size and interplanar spacing.

R-CDs imaging properties. (A) R-CDs stability diagram during 90 days. (B) full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) distance spectrum of the R-CDs (C) Result of the cytotoxicity of R-CDs by MTT assay after 48 h incubation. ***indicated significant differences from the control at p < 0.01. (D) 2D fluorescent microscopy images of U-87 cells that treated with 60 μg/ml R-CDs.

Ellipsoid generation microfluidic chip design and properties. (A) The chip mold was drawn using SolidWorks software. (B) scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of chip mold that printed using lab-made PμSL 3D printer. (C) Chip master mold and fabricated chip. (D) cell seeding and ellipsoids formation in the chip. (E) The fluorescent images obtained from assessing the viability of ellipsoids on the 3rd- and 10th-day using AO/PI viability assay kit.

A) Fluorescence microscopy reveals spheroids labeled R-CDs, introduced after initial spheroid formation within the microfluidic chip. B). Fluorescent image of spheroids that U-87 cells treated with R-CDs before spheroid formation. C) Red fluorescent signals that obtained from R-CDs treated U-87 in direct co-culture ellipsoids with HUF cells. D) Core-shell co-culture ellipsoids, U-87 cells that treated with R-CDs formed core and HUF cells without any treatment formed shell.

head.ce:title: Next-generation 3D tumor modeling: A microfluidic platform with biocompatible red carbon dots for live cell imaging in co-cultured elongated spheroid tumor model

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departments of Microbiology and Microbial Biotechnology, Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Co-culture spheroids mimic tumor architecture more accurately than traditional 2D cell cultures, but non-invasive, long-term tracking of live cells within these 3D models remains a challenge. This study addresses this critical need by developing a novel approach for live cell imaging in U-87/HUF co-culture spheroids. We introduce water-soluble, biocompatible red carbon dots (R-CDs) exhibiting exceptional stability and brightness (21% quantum yield) specifically designed for imaging within these 3D models. Furthermore, we designed a microfluidic chip with ellipsoid-shaped microwells to efficiently generate two distinct co-culture spheroid types: direct mixing and core-shell. R-CDs enabled non-invasive tracking of U-87 cancer cell location within these 3D models demonstrating their efficacy for long-term monitoring of live cells in cancer research. This R-CD and microfluidic technology has the potential to accelerate cancer drug discovery by enabling live cell studies in 3D tumor models.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent advances in microscopy have greatly improved our ability to understand how cells work, providing clearer and more detailed views of cellular structures and processes than ever before (Ettinger and Wittmann, 2014) live-cell imaging has become a crucial tool in various fields of biological and medical research, such as developmental biology, neurobiology, and oncology. This innovative approach allows real-time monitoring of dynamic cellular activities such as cell division, organelle movement, protein interactions and cell fate (Hallaji et al., 2022; Jensen, 2013) Fluorescent probe such as organic dyes and fluorescent proteins are indispensable tools in live cell imaging, enabling a wide range of applications, from assessing cell viability and proliferation to tracking cell dynamics and interactions. Cytoplasmic staining dyes, in particular, provide critical insights into cellular processes. While some commercial dyes like CellBrite excel in long-term cell tracking, challenges such as photobleaching, cytotoxicity, spectral overlap, and photostability often hinder their utility (Duan et al., 2023; Fernández-Suárez and Ting, 2008; Hallaji et al., 2022). Moreover, the production of specific fluorescent protein can be time-consuming (Duan et al., 2023) and expensive. Therefore, careful dye selection and optimization are paramount for reliable and accurate long-term live cell imaging experiments.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of carbon-based nanoparticle with size ranges from 1 to 10 nm. First discovered in 2004 by Xu et al. during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (Durrani et al., 2022; Wang and Lu, 2022). CDs have emerged as promising tools for fluorescent live cell imaging to their unique properties such as bright and photostable fluorescence, excellent water solubility, low cytotoxicity, stimulus-responsive fluorescence, and red and near infrared emission (Hallaji et al., 2023). These characteristics collectively make carbon dots valuable tools for enhancing imaging applications in biological and medical research, including live-cell imaging, drug delivery, and image-guided therapies. One key advantage of carbon dots with red emission (R-CDs) over those with blue or green emission is their superior efficiency. Red-shifted emission is particularly beneficial because it can penetrate tissues more effectively, causing minimal optical damage and providing clearer, more reliable imaging in complex biological environments (Warjurkar et al., 2023). Among the various types of R-CDs, those with high water solubility, low cytotoxicity and exceptional quantum yield are of particular interest, driving significant efforts in their synthesis due to their valuable applications in imaging (Hallaji et al., 2021).

3D cell culture methods are proposed as an alternative due to their potential to imitate cell-cell and cell-extracellular matrix interactions, thereby creating an environment that closely resembles in-vivo conditions (Hira et al., 2020; Kapałczyńska et al., 2018). Spheroid models are one of the most prevalent 3D cell culture approaches as their structure and morphology bear close resemblance to in-vivo tumors and produce more accurate results in cancer research (Haycock, 2011; Kitel et al., 2013; Lazzari et al., 2018). Among the various methods for creating 3D models, including suspension cultures, hanging drop methods, ultralow attachment plates, and magnetic levitation, microfluidic chips are a preferred choice due to their ability to regulate the flow of nutrients and excretory substances, resulting in spheroids of uniform size and homogeneity, as well as enabling high-throughput assays (Meyvantsson and Beebe, 2008) (Moshksayan et al., 2018; Van Duinen et al., 2015). Additionally, microfluidic chips allow for precise control of cellular behavior by mimicking the in-vivo microenvironment, and their miniaturized size, reduces the number of reagents and cells required, making them a cost-effective and efficient option for 3D cell culture (Ahvaraki et al., 2024; Lee et al., 2013; Ruppen et al., 2014; Vadivelu et al., 2017; Vakhshiteh et al., 2023).

Fluorescent live imaging techniques using carbon dots (CDs) offer practical, cost-effective, and non-toxic methods to gain significant information on cancer cells without disrupting their activity and signaling pathways (Hallaji et al., 2022; Phan and Cho, 2022).

However, the application of R-CDs in 3D cell culture models, particularly for tracking cells within co-culture systems, remains largely unexplored. In this study, we addressed this gap by using R-CDs to track live cells in 3D co-culture tumor spheroids. To achieve this, we designed a microfluidic chip that enables the formation of a novel elongated spheroid, or "Ellipsoid." Using these Ellipsoid spheroids, we established two co-culture models—direct mixing and core-shell co-culture—to explore their potential applications. The R-CDs, synthesized through a solvothermal method using citric acid, urea, and formic acid, exhibited excellent brightness, minimal cytotoxicity, high solubility, making them ideal for long-term live-cell imaging. To test their practical application, we labeled U-87 glioblastoma cells with R-CDs and co-cultured them with human uterine fibroblast (HUF) cells inside the microfluidic chip, allowing for a detailed study of cell interactions in a controlled 3D environment.

Citric acid, urea, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) were purchased from MERCK (Germany). Formic acid (99.9%) was obtained from CHEMLAB (Belgium), while MTT powder was sourced from BIOBASIC (Canada). The PDMS Sylgard 184 Elastomer Kit was acquired from Dow Corning (USA). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), penicillin/streptomycin, and trypsin/EDTA were purchased from GIBCO. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from DNA Biotech, and ethanol (99%) from Mojallali (Iran). Finally, the Acridine Orange (AO)/Propidium Iodide (PI) viability assay kit was sourced from Q. MEDX (Iran).

R-CDs were synthesized using a solvothermal method. In brief, 1 g of urea, 0.5 g of citric acid, and 5 mL of formic acid were combined in a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. These specific amounts were selected based on prior optimization to ensure a complete reaction and maximize yield. After heating, the resulting red-brown solution was purified by ethanol washing, centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 20 min (three times), and filtration through a 0.22-μm membrane to remove large particles. The solution was then dialyzed in a 1000 Da bag for 48 h at 4 °C to achieve a neutral pH. Finally, the purified product was freeze-dried into a powder for storage. Fig. 1 provides a schematic of the key synthesis and purification steps.

To fully characterize the optical properties and understand the structure of the synthesized red carbon dots (R-CDs), various analytical techniques were employed. Schimadzu Analytik Jena ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy was used to measure the light absorption properties of the R-CDs. For determining the photoluminescence spectra, Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrometer (Agilent) was employed. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were captured using an HR-TEM FEI Tecni F20 microscope. To gain further insight into the chemical functionalities present on the surface of the R-CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed using a Thermo Nicolet FT-IR spectrophotometer. Finally, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the R-CDs was obtained using a diffractometer in the 2θ range of 10°-80°.

The MTT assay was used to assess R-CD cytotoxicity on U-87 cells. In this assay, U-87 cells that had been previously exposed to different concentrations of R-CDs during 48 h were incubated with a solution of MTT reagent (0.5 mg/mL) for 3 h at 37 °C. Following incubation, the formazan crystals were then dissolved using DMSO. After a brief incubation period to ensure complete solubilization, the absorbance of the resulting solution was measured at two specific wavelengths: 570 nm (primary wavelength) and 630 nm (reference wavelength) using a microplate reader. The ratio of absorbance values at these wavelengths provides a quantitative measure of cell viability.

The microfluidic chip fabrication process builds upon the methods described in our previous reports (Behroodi et al., 2020; Ermis et al., 2022), with the key innovation being the design of the microwells. These microwells have a unique conical shape with an elliptical cross-section, achieved using a specially designed mold printed with a high-resolution lab-made PμSL 3D printer. After printing, the mold is coated with a polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) solution. A degassed mixture of PDMS prepolymer and curing agent in a 10:1 ratio is then poured onto the PMMA-coated mold, ensuring proper curing and replication of the microfluidic features. The PDMS is cured at 90 °C for an hour, after which inlet and outlet channels are precisely punched. Two PDMS layers are then permanently bonded using plasma treatment, followed by an additional curing step at 90 °C for 6 h to ensure a robust, leak-proof microfluidic chip. Before use, the chip is sterilized with ultraviolet radiation for 20 min. Finally, the sterilized chip is coated with a 1% PVA solution for 24 h to promote the formation of a compact, spheroid tumor model within the microwells. PVA plays a critical role in altering surface properties by hindering the adhesion of functional groups to the PDMS surface.

The human glioblastoma cancer cell line U-87 and human fibroblast cell line HUF, obtained from the PASTOR institute in Iran, were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 1% penicillin/streptomycin antibiotic and 10% FBS. These cells were incubated at 37 °C and 5% CO2 until they reached 80% confluency.

For mono-culture ellipsoid formation, after the cells expanded, they were detached and a 100 μl suspension containing 1 million cells (1 × 106 cells) was seeded into the microfluidic chip. The chip was then placed into an incubator for 48 h to allow the formation of compact ellipsoid.

To demonstrate the potential of R-CDs for cell labeling during co-culture preparation, we investigate two models: direct mixing and core-shell (Vakhshiteh et al., 2023). In the direct mixing approach, U-87 cancer cells are first exposed to R-CDs for 24 h in a T25. Following trypsinization and washing with fresh culture medium to remove unbound R-CDs, half a million of these washed cells are counted, mixed with an equal number of HUF cells, and then introduced into the microfluidic chip and then incubation was done for 48 h (Fig. 2 A).

To create co-cultured bilayer core-shell models, 0.5 × 106 U-87 cells treated with R-CDs were introduced into the microfluidic chip. After 24 h of incubation and core formation, 0.5 × 106 fresh HUF cells without the R-CDs treatment were infused to the chip and incubated for 48 h to produce the shell of the ellipsoid (Fig. 2B).

Highly fluorescent, water-dispersible, and biocompatible R-CDs were fabricated using a solvothermal synthesis method. Fig. 3 A illustrates the appearance of R-CDs under UV and visible light. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3B) reveals two absorption peaks at approximately 460 nm and 540 nm. Fluorescence spectroscopy experiments, with the R-CDs excited at these two wavelengths, resulted in distinct emission peaks at 540 and 630 nm, respectively.

HR-TEM analysis of synthesized R-CDs (Fig. 3F) revealed their morphology. The presence of individual crystalline zones within the R-CDs, indicated by circles in the figure, displayed a characteristic interplanar spacing of 3 Å, suggesting a partially graphitic structure. Additionally, the HR-TEM images indicated a uniform size distribution, averaging between 4 and 6 nm.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern in Fig. 3D shows the crystalline structure of the analyzed material. The graph highlights prominent peaks at 2θ2\theta2θ values of around 26° and 44°, corresponding to the (002) and (001) crystallographic planes. The (002) peak suggests a partially graphitic structure, indicating regular spacing between graphene layers. The sharpness and intensity of the peaks reflect high crystallinity, revealing well-defined layers within the material.

The observed FTIR peaks of the synthesized carbon dots can be assigned to specific functional groups present on the carbon dot structure (Fig. 3E). The observed peaks at specific wavenumbers (cm−1) confirm the presence of these functional groups: O–H stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups (3331.92), N–H stretching vibrations of amine groups (3140.93), C–H stretching vibrations (2802.41), C=O stretching vibrations (1762.27), the peak at 1562.084 cm⁻1 could be associated with N–H bending, The peaks at 1363.448 cm⁻1 and 1234.485 cm⁻1 suggest the presence of C–H bending or C–O stretching, commonly found in methyl groups or ethers, while the peak at 1191.485 cm⁻1 may also point to C–O or C–N stretching. This likely contributes to the water solubility of the R-CDs, as these hydrophilic groups readily interact with water molecules.

The R-CDs exhibited a remarkably high quantum efficiency (QY) of approximately 21% for the absorption peak at 540 nm, measured using Rhodamine 6G as a reference standard. This value is significantly higher than what is typically observed for water-soluble R-CDs. Additionally, the R-CDs demonstrated unique stability, making them suitable for imaging applications. To assess the optical stability of the synthesized R-CDs, their fluorescence intensity was measured over a period of 90 days. The resulting graph Fig. 4 A indicates the emission intensity of sample that measured immediately after synthesis dose not a significant difference, compared to the emission intensity of 90 days sample.

The other fluorescence features of R-CDs, characterized by a remarkably narrow full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of 53 nm and a long Stokes shift of 75 nm, are exceptionally well-suited for live cell imaging (Fig. 4B). A 53 nm FWHM provides a good balance between spectral resolution and brightness. The 75 nm Stokes shift reduces background noise, improves the signal-to-noise ratio, and enhances multiplexing capability when using with other dyes by minimizing spectral overlap (Abeywickrama, 2022; Jiang et al., 2023). These features make the prepared R-CDs ideal for live and bright imaging.

The cytotoxicity of R-CDs towards U-87 glioblastoma cells was evaluated using a standard MTT assay Fig. 4C. Five thousand U-87 cells were seeded per well in a 96-well plate and incubated for 48 h. Subsequently, varying concentrations of R-CDs (4 μg/mL to 2000 μg/mL) were added, with each concentration replicated four times to ensure data accuracy. Interestingly, no significant difference in cell proliferation was observed even at high R-CD concentrations compared to untreated control cells. Notably, cell viability remained above 90% even after 48 and 72 h of incubation with R-CDs at concentrations as high as 125 μg/mL. These findings suggest that R-CDs may not exhibit cytotoxic effects on U-87 cells under these experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Live-cell imaging of monolayers using R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the R-CDs' suitability for live cell imaging, U-87 cells were incubated with R-CDs (60 μg/mL) for 24 h, followed by washing and visualization under a fluorescence microscope. Fig. 4D demonstrates the successful cellular uptake of R-CDs with strong fluorescence emission, indicating their potential for live cell imaging without apparent cytotoxicity or changing the morphology of cells.

The geometry of tumors affects how drugs penetrate and distribute within tumor tissue. As tumors transition from 2D culture to 3D structures like spheroids, the surface area-to-volume (S/V) ratio decreases. This is critical because cells in a 2D environment have a higher S/V ratio, enhancing drug exposure and uptake, but this does not reflect the complex environment of solid tumors. Spheroid models, with lower S/V ratios, experience reduced drug accessibility, creating drug concentration gradients (Dickinson and Lele, 2023; Sefidgar et al., 2014).

To enhance drug sensitivity studies, it is essential to create tumor models that mimic natural shapes. Developing different shapes (e.g., spheroids, ellipsoids) helps study how geometry affects drug distribution and efficacy, improving predictions of drug performance in vivo. We present a novel microfluidic chip with an elliptical design of microwell that facilitates the creation and maintenance of uniformly sized ellipsoids. Moreover, in this new design, the ellipsoids maintain their structure for more than 10 days compared to 5 days for spheroids, making this model more suitable for drug evaluation studies. This extended lifespan makes it ideal for studying tumor progression and response to drug treatments over time, enabling more robust drug evaluation and resistance analysis.

Fig. 5 A and C illustrates the designed mold geometry, corresponding 3D-printed mold and the PDMS molding process. To validate the chip's efficacy, we assessed ellipsoid viability using an acridine orange and propidium iodide (AO-PI) viability assay on days 3 and 10. Fluorescence microscopy revealed a high percentage of viable cells within this new chip. Notably, the intensity of red PI staining (indicating dead cells) did not differ significantly between three and 10-day-old tumors, with minimal red fluorescence observed in both groups. These findings confirm the efficacy of the designed chip and its ability to support high cell survival in 3D cell culture for extended periods (Fig. 5D and E).

Fig. S1 compares the S/V ratios of 2D, 3D spheroid, and 3D ellipsoid cell cultures. The 2D cell culture has a S/V ratio of 1.20 μm⁻1, for simplified by assuming a rounded single cell with a diameter of 5 μm. However, the real S/V ratio of cells in 2D cell culture likely much larger. The spheroid has a lower S/V ratio of 0.0257 μm⁻1 with a diameter of 233 μm, and the ellipsoid has an even lower S/V ratio of 0.00859 μm⁻1, with dimensions of 233
× 233 ×
357 μm. the 2D culture has an S/V ratio approximately 46 times greater than the spheroid, and the spheroid's S/V ratio is roughly 3 times greater than that of the ellipsoid. This variation impacts drug penetration and distribution, with 3D cultures providing a more accurate representation of drug accessibility within solid tumors.

The impact of R-CDs on cell aggregation and spheroid/ellipsoid formation was assessed using two different approaches (Fig. 6 A and B). In the first approach, pre-formed ellipsoids within the chip were incubated with R-CDs (60 μg/mL) for 24 h (Fig. 6A). In this case, fluorescence signal primarily originated from the outer layers exposed to the R-CDs and the imaging resolution is not appropriate. In the second method, U-87 cells were pre-treated with R-CDs (60 μg/mL) for 24 h. The cells were then detached, washed with PBS, and infused into the microfluidic device. After 48 h within the chip, the resulting ellipsoids displayed strong fluorescence throughout their structure, indicating effective penetration of R-CDs and suggesting potentially enhanced analysis of deeper cell layers. Conversely, (Fig. 6B). Overall, these findings demonstrate that R-CDs do not interfere with cell aggregation to form ellipsoids. Since the aggregation of cells into spheroids/ellipsoids is a multifaceted process involving cellular motility, adhesion, ECM interactions, inter- and intracellular signaling pathways, and morphological changes, preserving this process is crucial (Ma et al., 2022). Therefore, the development of a non-toxic dye capable of staining living cells without disrupting their normal functions signifies a substantial advancement in live cell imaging of 3D cell culture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell tracking in direct mixing co-culture ellipsoids using R-CDs as the fluorescent prob
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the advantageous imaging properties of R-CDs discussed earlier, we employed them as fluorescent probes to track U-87 glioblastoma cells within co-cultured ellipsoids. U-87 cells were pre-labeled with R-CDs before being directly mixed with HUF fibroblasts to facilitate ellipsoid formation. As shown in Fig. 6C, the distribution of U-87 cells relative to HUF fibroblasts in the co-cultured ellipsoids is visualized by the red fluorescent signal. The analysis revealed that a majority of U-87 cells reside in the inner regions of the co-cultured ellipsoids, while HUF cells predominantly occupy the outer layers. This spatial distribution is further supported by the minimal red fluorescence detected in the external areas of the ellipsoid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell tracking in core-shell U-87/HUF ellipsoids using R-CDs as the fluorescent prob
ce:section.ce:para: R-CDs were successfully employed as fluorescent probes to track U-87 cells within core-shell co-cultured ellipsoids (Fig. 6D). U-87 cells pre-treated with R-CDs formed the core (inner layer) of the ellipsoids, while HUF fibroblast cells formed the outer shell. The distribution of U-87 cells was confirmed by the strong red fluorescence signal localized within the core region. Furthermore, the size comparison between brightfield and fluorescence microscopy images revealed a clear distinction between the core and shell layers, signifying minimal R-CD penetration into the outer shell. Importantly, the R-CDs did not affect the overall structural integrity of the ellipsoids, demonstrating their suitability for long-term cellular imaging without compromising the viability or function of the cells.

We developed a novel microfluidic platform to generate highly reproducible, heterotypic ellipsoid-shaped 3D tumor models that more accurately mimic in vivo tumor architecture compared to traditional spheroids. To visualize and quantify cellular distribution within these complex structures, we synthesized highly stable, red-emissive carbon dots with suitable quantum yield and biocompatibility. These carbon dots effectively labeled cells without impairing viability, cell-cell interactions, or aggregation, enabling long-term tracking of cell fate and distribution in two different co-culture tumor models: core-shell and direct mixing. Our findings highlight the utility of carbon dots as powerful tools for live cell imaging in complex 3D models. By focusing on this application, we circumvent the need for precise molecular details, a challenge that has previously hindered the commercialization of carbon dots for biomedical applications. While this study offers valuable insights into using carbon dots for live cell imaging in 3D tumor models, further research is needed to fully understand carbon dot-cell interactions and explore their potential for drug delivery and therapy.

Parisa Pournemat: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Zeinab Bagheri: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ebrahim Behroodi: Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Marziye Soleimani: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Hamid Latifi: Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Shadi Mayadani: Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Mohammad Yaghoubi-Avini: Resources, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support provided by Shahid Beheshti University of Iran.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2024.116684.
